intro: The Musk v. Altman trial dominated the week, with explosive testimony and ominous texts making headlines, while a wave of security vulnerabilities — from a critical Linux bug to mass cPanel exploitation — reminded everyone that the attack surface keeps growing.

stories.title: Week one of the Musk v. Altman trial: threats, goblin admissions, and AGI fears
stories.summary: The first week of the landmark Musk v. OpenAI trial in Oakland played out like a Silicon Valley drama with genuine legal teeth. Elon Musk took the stand in a black suit and argued that Sam Altman and Greg Brockman had deceived him into bankrolling OpenAI in its early days, framing the nonprofit-to-for-profit transition as a betrayal of the founding mission. In a stunning moment, Musk admitted under questioning that xAI has been distilling — essentially learning from — OpenAI's own models, which somewhat undermines his moral high ground in the case. OpenAI's legal team hit back hard, revealing that Musk had texted both Altman and Brockman warning they would be 'the most hated men in America' if the company didn't settle, a message OpenAI characterized as coercion. Musk's prior 'World War III' threat from his Twitter lawsuit also resurfaced and was used to paint a pattern of threatening behavior. Musk's sole expert witness, longtime AI researcher Stuart Russell, testified that he fears an AGI arms race and believes governments need to step in and restrain frontier labs — a position that ironically lends some credibility to OpenAI's own safety framing. The trial has become a proxy battle over whether OpenAI's commercialization constitutes a breach of its original charitable mission. For the broader AI industry, the outcome could have real consequences for how nonprofit-to-for-profit conversions are structured going forward. It's also a rare moment where two of the most powerful figures in AI are forced to air their private communications in open court. Whatever you think of either party, the testimony is revealing a lot about how the AI industry actually operates behind closed doors.

stories.title: Influential ChatGPT-in-education study retracted after already being cited hundreds of times
stories.summary: A high-profile academic study touting the benefits of ChatGPT in educational settings has been retracted after reviewers raised significant red flags about its methodology and data integrity. The study had already accumulated hundreds of citations, meaning its flawed conclusions have propagated through the academic literature and likely influenced policy discussions, curriculum decisions, and further research. The retraction is a textbook example of how quickly questionable AI research can embed itself into the conversation before scrutiny catches up. The specific red flags haven't been fully disclosed, but they were serious enough to warrant full retraction rather than a correction. This matters because the education sector is one of the most active areas of AI deployment right now, and policymakers and school administrators are actively looking for evidence to justify adoption decisions. Bad evidence spreading fast is arguably worse than no evidence at all. It also raises questions about peer review processes in the AI era — the pace of publication is simply outrunning the pace of rigorous review. For anyone building AI-in-education tools or evaluating them, this is a useful reminder to be skeptical of studies that seem too clean and too confirmatory. The broader lesson is that the AI research literature needs much stronger editorial standards before it starts shaping real-world institutions. Expect this to fuel calls for pre-registration and more robust replication requirements in AI-adjacent research.

stories.title: Image AI models now drive app growth more than chatbot upgrades — but monetization lags
stories.summary: New data from Appfigures finds that launching a visual AI model generates roughly 6.5 times more app downloads than releasing a chatbot upgrade, marking a notable shift in what actually moves the needle for consumer AI products. The pattern suggests that image generation has become the primary user acquisition hook in the AI app market, displacing the conversational AI hype that dominated 2023 and 2024. However, the data also shows a stark conversion gap — most apps that see a download spike from a visual model launch fail to convert that surge into sustained revenue. This points to a fundamental monetization problem: people are excited to try AI image tools but aren't willing to pay for them at the rate necessary to justify the compute costs. The pattern mirrors what happened with early chatbot apps, where downloads were high but retention and willingness-to-pay were low. For developers and investors in the AI app space, this is useful signal that visual AI is currently the best top-of-funnel strategy, but it doesn't solve the bottom-of-funnel problem. The companies that figure out the retention and monetization layer — not just the flash of a new model — will be the ones that survive. It also suggests the market is still in an exploration phase where users are sampling rather than committing. If you're building in this space, the data argues for investing heavily in the post-download experience rather than chasing the next model release.

stories.title: OpenAI and PwC partner to automate CFO workflows with AI agents
stories.summary: OpenAI has announced a collaboration with PwC to bring AI agents into enterprise finance operations, specifically targeting the office of the CFO. The partnership is aimed at automating workflows like financial forecasting, variance analysis, audit preparation, and internal controls monitoring — tasks that are currently labor-intensive and prone to human error. PwC will serve as both an implementation partner and a distribution channel, using its existing relationships with large enterprises to deploy OpenAI's agent infrastructure. This is part of a broader OpenAI push into enterprise services that also includes its recently announced partnership with AWS and its FedRAMP Moderate authorization. For enterprise buyers, the PwC angle is significant because it provides a trusted intermediary who can handle the compliance, customization, and change management work that raw API access doesn't address. Finance is a particularly high-stakes domain for AI agents because errors can have material consequences, so the framing around 'strengthening controls' is doing real work in the pitch. The collaboration signals that OpenAI is increasingly thinking of itself as a platform company that sells through partners, not just a model company that sells direct. For the Big Four consulting firms, this is also a strategic necessity — they need to embed themselves in AI workflows now or risk being disintermediated. Whether AI agents actually improve CFO-level decision-making at scale is still an open empirical question, but the commercial momentum is clearly there.

stories.title: OpenAI details how it rebuilt its WebRTC stack for low-latency voice AI at scale
stories.summary: OpenAI published a detailed technical post explaining how it rebuilt its WebRTC infrastructure to power real-time Voice AI with low latency and global scale. The post goes into the architecture decisions behind their voice stack, including how they handle conversational turn-taking — the tricky problem of knowing when a user has stopped speaking and the AI should respond. Low latency in voice AI is non-negotiable for a natural user experience; even a few hundred milliseconds of extra delay makes conversations feel stilted and robotic. OpenAI's approach involved rethinking the entire signaling and media path rather than layering improvements on top of a legacy WebRTC setup. The post is unusually transparent for OpenAI and reads as both a technical flex and a recruitment signal. For developers building voice AI products or WebRTC applications, there's genuine engineering insight here about the tradeoffs involved in real-time AI inference pipelines. The piece also implicitly acknowledges that voice is a major strategic frontier for OpenAI — it's the interface layer that makes AI feel most human. Hacker News picked this up with over 360 points, reflecting strong developer interest in the underlying infrastructure details. As voice AI becomes more embedded in products ranging from customer service to coding assistants, understanding how to build the plumbing reliably at scale becomes a competitive differentiator.

stories.title: IBM's Granite 4.1 family of open LLMs lands with Apache 2.0 license in 3B, 8B, and 30B sizes
stories.summary: IBM released the Granite 4.1 family of language models a few days ago, and they're notable primarily for their Apache 2.0 license — one of the most permissive available, allowing commercial use without the restrictions that come with Meta's Llama license, for example. The family comes in 3B, 8B, and 30B parameter sizes, making it practical for on-device or resource-constrained deployments as well as more capable server-side applications. Simon Willison did a quick test of the 3B model's ability to generate SVG images of pelicans — a standard informal benchmark in the LLM community for evaluating small model visual reasoning and code generation — and found it reasonably capable for its size class. The Apache 2.0 licensing is the real headline here for enterprise developers who need clean IP provenance for production deployments. IBM has been steadily building out its open model portfolio as part of its enterprise AI strategy, positioning Granite as the model family for customers who want open-weight models with clear commercial terms. The 30B variant puts it in direct competition with other mid-size open models like Llama 3.1 70B (at roughly half the parameter count) and various Mistral variants. For developers who've been waiting for a well-resourced open model with no licensing ambiguity, Granite 4.1 is worth evaluating. The training details published by the Granite team give reasonable transparency into how the models were built, which is increasingly a factor in enterprise trust decisions.

stories.title: Canadian election databases used 'canary traps' to catch leaks — and it worked
stories.summary: Canada's election infrastructure quietly employed a classic counterintelligence technique called a 'canary trap' in its voter databases — embedding intentional, unique errors in copies of the data given to different parties or individuals. When the specific error in a leaked copy is identified, it points directly to which copy was compromised, and therefore who leaked it. The technique has a long history in intelligence and publishing circles (publishers sometimes plant unique typos in advance copies to track leaks), but its use in electoral databases is a notable operational security move. The fact that it worked — successfully identifying the source of a database leak — validates the approach for other democratic institutions thinking about how to protect sensitive electoral data. Election database security has become a serious concern in the era of influence operations and foreign interference, and canary traps offer a relatively low-cost way to add accountability without changing how the data is used. The story is a useful reminder that some of the most effective security techniques are social and procedural rather than technical. For security engineers and architects managing sensitive data shared across multiple parties, the canary trap principle is worth understanding and potentially applying. It's also a story about operational tradecraft becoming relevant infrastructure policy, which is an interesting convergence.

stories.title: Oscars bar AI-generated actors and writing from award eligibility
stories.summary: The Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences has issued new award eligibility rules that explicitly exclude AI-generated performances and AI-authored scripts from Oscar consideration. This is a significant institutional line-drawing moment in an industry that has been grappling with both the creative and labor implications of generative AI. The rules create a formal definition of 'human authorship' for the purposes of the most prestigious film awards, which will likely have downstream effects on how studios approach AI use in production. The move follows pressure from writers' and actors' unions, who have been fighting for AI protections since the 2023 strikes. For the practical question of what counts as AI-generated versus AI-assisted, the details of the eligibility criteria will matter enormously — and the Academy will likely face hard judgment calls as the technology becomes more embedded in workflows. The ruling doesn't ban AI from filmmaking; it just says AI-primary work won't be honored. That's a meaningful distinction that leaves significant room for AI as a production tool while protecting human creative credit. Similar discussions are happening in music (Spotify's new Verified badge for human artists) and literature, suggesting a broader cultural reckoning with how we attribute and value human creative work in the AI era.

stories.title: Trump administration invokes national security to stall 165 onshore wind farm projects
stories.summary: The Trump administration has cited national security grounds to block or pause 165 onshore wind farm projects across the United States, effectively bringing a large portion of new wind energy development to a standstill. This follows earlier executive actions targeting offshore wind, and together they represent a sweeping effort to slow renewable energy buildout through executive action rather than legislation. The national security framing is legally useful because it gives agencies broad discretionary authority and is harder to challenge in court than regulatory rulemakings. Critics argue the security rationale is pretextual and that the real driver is ideological opposition to wind energy and support for fossil fuel industries. For the energy industry, the immediate effect is significant uncertainty for project developers, investors, and grid planners who have been building capacity projections around approved wind projects. Data centers and large tech companies with renewable energy commitments are indirectly affected, as the supply of clean energy to match their consumption is constrained. The broader infrastructure and climate implications are substantial — wind has been one of the fastest-growing sources of new electricity generation capacity in the U.S. Whether these stalls survive legal challenge remains to be seen, but the regulatory risk premium on renewable energy projects in the U.S. just went up considerably.

stories.title: Spotify launches 'Verified' badges to distinguish human artists from AI-generated music
stories.summary: Spotify has begun rolling out 'Verified' badges for artists to signal that they are human creators rather than AI-generated profiles. The verification process will review factors like live performance history, social media presence, and other signals of authentic human artistic activity. This is a direct response to the growing problem of AI-generated music flooding streaming platforms, diluting royalty pools, and making it harder for human artists to be discovered. The AI music deluge has been a genuine operational problem for streaming services — it's relatively cheap to generate thousands of songs and upload them under fake artist names to accumulate streams and collect micro-royalties. Spotify's verification approach mirrors what Twitter/X and other platforms have done for identity verification, but applied to the question of human creative authorship. The system will inevitably face edge cases: what about a human artist who uses AI tools heavily? What about AI systems operated by humans with genuine artistic intent? These definitional questions will get harder as the technology improves. For human artists and the music industry broadly, the badge is a meaningful signal that Spotify is taking their concerns seriously, even if the enforcement mechanism is still being worked out. It also connects to the Oscars' simultaneous move to define and protect human creative credit, suggesting a broader institutional convergence on this question.

stories.title: GameStop bids $56 billion for eBay — without a clear plan for how to pay
stories.summary: In what may be the most audacious corporate longshot of the year, GameStop has submitted a $56 billion takeover offer for eBay. The offer is extraordinary on its face: GameStop has been closing stores, bleeding revenue, and is a fraction of the size of eBay. The company hasn't provided a credible explanation of how it would finance the acquisition, which is raising obvious eyebrows from analysts and investors alike. GameStop's meme stock status — it still carries an outsized retail investor following from the 2021 short squeeze — means the announcement itself can move markets and generate headlines, which may be part of the strategic calculus. eBay's market cap and revenue profile are simply not in the same universe as GameStop's current financial position. This reads less like a serious M&A play and more like an attention-generating maneuver, perhaps to talk up the stock price or signal some kind of strategic pivot to investors. For the broader market, it's a reminder that meme-adjacent companies can still make moves that force serious analysis even when the underlying logic is thin. eBay, for its part, has not indicated any interest in the offer. If GameStop is genuinely trying to transform itself into some kind of marketplace or e-commerce entity, there are cheaper and more realistic ways to do that than acquiring a company ten times your size.

stories.title: Cerebras IPO on track for $26.6 billion+ valuation, with deep OpenAI ties
stories.summary: AI chip maker Cerebras Systems is heading toward what looks like a blockbuster IPO, with indications pointing to a valuation of $26.6 billion or higher. The company makes wafer-scale processors — essentially chips the size of an entire silicon wafer rather than the small dies that are then packaged together — optimized for AI inference and training workloads. Its relationship with OpenAI is described as 'deep and rich,' with OpenAI serving as both a customer and a strategic partner, which gives Cerebras significant revenue visibility and credibility ahead of the offering. The IPO comes at a moment of intense investor appetite for anything in the AI chip supply chain, with Nvidia's dominance making people eager for alternatives. Cerebras has positioned itself as a high-throughput inference play, particularly for workloads where latency matters — like real-time voice AI and agentic applications. The company previously filed for an IPO but faced scrutiny over its relationship with a large Middle Eastern customer; it's unclear how that dynamic has evolved. At $26.6B+, this would be one of the larger tech IPOs in recent memory and would give Cerebras significant capital to compete in a market where scale and R&D investment are everything. For investors, the key question is whether Cerebras can hold its niche as Nvidia continues to iterate and as other competitors like AMD and Intel make their own inference-focused moves.

stories.title: Sierra raises $950M to become the 'global standard' for enterprise AI customer experiences
stories.summary: Sierra, the enterprise AI customer experience company co-founded by Bret Taylor and Clay Bavor, has raised $950 million in a new funding round, giving it over $1 billion in total capital to work with. The company is building AI-powered customer service and engagement systems for large enterprises, competing in a space that includes Salesforce, ServiceNow, and a dozen well-funded startups. The stated ambition — to become the 'global standard for AI-powered customer experiences' — is the kind of bold framing you expect from a round of this size. Sierra's founding team has serious pedigree: Taylor was co-CEO of Salesforce and chairman of Twitter's board during the Musk acquisition saga, and Bavor was a senior Google executive. The enterprise AI market is intensely competitive right now, with customers often deploying multiple point solutions rather than standardizing on a single platform. Sierra's bet is that enterprises will eventually consolidate on a purpose-built AI experience layer rather than stitching together general-purpose models. The $950M raise gives them runway to build out the sales, implementation, and customer success infrastructure that enterprise sales requires. Whether the 'global standard' vision materializes will depend heavily on whether their AI actually outperforms what enterprises can build themselves on top of OpenAI or Anthropic APIs — which is a real open question.

stories.title: Fervo Energy files for IPO targeting up to $1.3B raise at $6.5B valuation
stories.summary: Enhanced geothermal startup Fervo Energy has filed for an IPO looking to raise up to $1.3 billion, which would value the company at up to $6.5 billion. Fervo uses directional drilling techniques borrowed from the oil and gas industry to access geothermal heat in locations where traditional geothermal isn't viable, dramatically expanding the geographic reach of geothermal power. Unlike solar and wind, geothermal provides firm, always-on baseload power — a property that's becoming increasingly valuable as grids struggle with the intermittency of other renewables. The timing of the IPO is notable: it comes as AI data center demand for reliable, 24/7 clean power is creating a genuine premium for always-on renewable sources. Fervo has existing power purchase agreements with Google, among others, who need to match their AI compute energy consumption with clean power that's available around the clock, not just when the wind blows. The company's technology is genuinely novel and has demonstrated commercial viability, which sets it apart from many cleantech IPOs that go public on promise rather than performance. The $6.5B valuation reflects both the technical achievement and the strategic timing in a market hungry for grid-scale clean baseload. For the energy transition broadly, a successful Fervo IPO would provide validation and capital to a category that's been underfunded relative to its potential.

stories.title: Bun JavaScript runtime is being ported from Zig to Rust — and people have feelings about it
stories.summary: A commit to the Bun repository confirmed that the JavaScript runtime is being ported from Zig — the language it was famously built in — to Rust, triggering a significant reaction from the developer community on Hacker News (370+ points on the announcement, 460+ on the worried response piece). Bun was positioned partly as a showcase for Zig's capabilities as a systems programming language suitable for high-performance runtimes, so the move to Rust carries symbolic weight beyond just an implementation detail. The practical reasons for the switch likely include Rust's larger ecosystem, better tooling, more mature memory safety guarantees, and a larger pool of potential contributors. However, the concern piece 'I am worried about Bun' reflects anxiety about whether the port signals instability, scope creep, or a fundamental rethinking of the project's direction. Zig's creator Kelsey Zig (known as 'Ziggy') and the Zig community have built significant mindshare around Bun as a proof point, so the move may dampen enthusiasm for Zig in production systems. For developers depending on Bun for production workloads, the key questions are: how long will the port take, will performance characteristics change, and will API stability be maintained during the transition? Rust ports of high-performance tools have generally gone well historically — see Ripgrep, Deno's partial Rust adoption — but the scope of rewriting a full runtime is substantial. The community reaction is worth watching as a signal of developer trust in Bun's long-term stability.

stories.title: Microsoft Edge stores all passwords in plaintext in memory, even when not in use
stories.summary: A researcher disclosed that Microsoft Edge stores all saved passwords in clear text in process memory, even passwords that aren't actively being used in the current session. This is a significant security issue because it means any process with memory read access — including malware, or even legitimate software with insufficient privilege boundaries — can trivially extract every saved password from a running Edge process. Modern password managers are generally expected to keep credentials encrypted in memory and only decrypt them on demand, minimizing the exposure window. Edge's behavior violates this principle by essentially keeping the full credential store decrypted at all times. The disclosure hit Hacker News with nearly 500 points, reflecting widespread concern and frustration from the security community. This is particularly awkward for Microsoft given its significant investment in security marketing and its push for enterprises to trust Edge as a managed browser. The practical mitigation for individuals and enterprises is to avoid using Edge's built-in password manager and instead use a dedicated password manager. Microsoft has not yet issued a formal response, fix timeline, or acknowledgment as of publication. For security-conscious organizations, this is the kind of finding that warrants a rapid internal review of Edge deployment policies.

stories.title: Unofficial 'Notepad++ for Mac' disavowed by original creator as vibe-coded fake
stories.summary: An app calling itself 'Notepad++ for Mac' appeared and started gaining attention, only for Notepad++'s creator Don Ho to publicly clarify that no official macOS version of Notepad++ exists or has ever been released. The unofficial app appears to have been built using AI-assisted 'vibe coding' — a term for generating functional-looking software quickly with AI assistance without deep engineering rigor. The creator's public disavowal is important because Notepad++ has a strong brand in the developer community, particularly among Windows power users, and an impersonator app risks both confusing users and potentially exposing them to security risks if the fake app includes malicious functionality. The episode is a small but concrete example of a problem that's going to get much larger: as AI makes it trivially easy to build plausible-looking apps, impersonation of well-known software tools becomes much easier. For users, the lesson is the familiar one: install software only from official sources and verified publishers, not from app stores or download sites that just happen to have the right name. For the open source community, the incident raises questions about brand protection for widely-used tools that don't have large corporate legal teams. Don Ho's quick and clear public response is the right move — the worst outcome would be a fake app accruing a large user base before the original creator weighs in.

stories.title: AMD adds HDMI 2.1 support to Linux, good news for the upcoming Steam Machine
stories.summary: AMD is contributing HDMI 2.1 support to the Linux kernel's AMDGPU driver, with Fixed Rate Link (FRL) support landing now and Display Stream Compression (DSC) coming soon after. This is directly relevant to Valve's upcoming Steam Machine — the rumored dedicated Linux gaming hardware that would essentially be a living-room PC running SteamOS. HDMI 2.1 enables 4K at 120Hz, 8K at 60Hz, and Variable Refresh Rate (VRR) over HDMI, which are all table-stakes features for a modern gaming device that needs to compete with PlayStation and Xbox. The fact that AMD is doing this upstream in the kernel rather than through proprietary drivers is good for the Linux gaming ecosystem broadly, not just for Valve's specific hardware plans. It also signals closer AMD-Valve collaboration, which has been a consistent theme over the past several years as the Steam Deck proved that AMD-powered Linux gaming hardware can find a real market. For Linux gamers using AMD GPUs on desktop systems, HDMI 2.1 support means they'll finally be able to fully utilize modern high-refresh-rate TVs and monitors over HDMI without dropping to DisplayPort. The timeline for DSC suggests this is an actively worked feature area rather than a one-off contribution. Overall, this is a quiet but meaningful infrastructure improvement that makes the Linux gaming stack more competitive with Windows.

stories.title: Critical 'CopyFail' Linux bug actively exploited in the wild — CISA issues warning
stories.summary: The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) has issued an active exploitation warning for a severe vulnerability dubbed 'CopyFail' (CVE-2026-31431) affecting major versions of the Linux kernel. The bug relates to a flaw in how Linux handles copy operations in the context of rootless containers, a configuration widely used in modern cloud and Kubernetes environments. Active exploitation means attackers aren't just theoretically capable of using this — they're actually using it right now in hacking campaigns, and the primary risk is to servers and data centers running Linux-based workloads, which is most of the internet's backend infrastructure. The detailed technical writeup on Hacker News (60 points, reasonably high signal) explains the specific interaction with rootless containers, which is the attack surface of concern. Rootless containers are increasingly common because they allow running containers without root privileges, which is a security improvement — except when a kernel bug specifically affects that code path. For infrastructure and DevOps teams, the immediate action items are: check your Linux kernel version against the affected range, apply vendor patches as they become available, and treat this as urgent if you're running containerized workloads in multi-tenant or internet-facing environments. The CISA advisory and active exploitation status mean this isn't something to defer to the next patch cycle.

stories.title: Hackers mass-exploiting critical cPanel vulnerability to compromise thousands of websites
stories.summary: Days after public disclosure of a critical vulnerability in cPanel and WHM — the web hosting control panel software used by millions of shared hosting accounts worldwide — attackers are actively exploiting it at scale to take over thousands of vulnerable websites. cPanel is deeply embedded in the shared hosting industry and is the control plane for both website files and email for a huge number of small businesses and personal sites. A critical vulnerability in cPanel is particularly dangerous because it often runs with elevated privileges, and compromising it can give an attacker full control over everything hosted on that server. The mass exploitation timeline — days after disclosure — is becoming unfortunately typical in the current threat landscape, where proof-of-concept exploits circulate almost immediately after CVE publication. For anyone running a website on shared hosting, the first check is whether your host uses cPanel and whether they've patched. Reputable hosting providers should have already applied the fix, but smaller or less-maintained hosts may lag. For security teams managing hosting infrastructure, this is an active incident that requires immediate patch verification and log review for indicators of compromise. The combination of this cPanel exploit and the CopyFail Linux kernel bug in the same news cycle is a useful reminder that the foundational infrastructure of the web is under constant, sophisticated attack.
